title: Scottish tablet
subtitle: Hard, grainy condensed milk candy, firmer and more crystalline than fudge
excerpt: A traditional Scottish confection cooked to the firm-ball stage and beaten until thick and grainy. Firmer and sweeter than fudge, with a crumbly, melt-on-the-tongue texture. Makes about 50 pieces.
sourceNotes: Scottish tablet is documented in Scottish household recipe collections from the early eighteenth century. It appears in Mistress Margaret Dods, The Cook and Housewife's Manual (1826), as a standard Scottish confection made from sugar and cream cooked to a hard set. Florence White, Good Things in England (1932), records tablet as a regional Scottish candy distinct from English fudge. The key distinction is a higher cooking temperature and a larger crystal size from less beating, producing a harder, more granular texture.

content.text: Tablet is not failed fudge. The
is the correct texture: crumbly and melting, exactly as it should be. The mixture sets fast once it starts to go. Have the tin ready lined before you start cooking, and don't leave the pan.

Line a 30 × 20 cm tray with baking paper. Combine the {{caster-sugar}}, {{condensed-milk}}, {{whole-milk}}, and {{unsalted-butter}} in a large, heavy-based saucepan. Heat gently, stirring, until the sugar dissolves. Bring to a boil and cook at a steady boil, stirring frequently, until the mixture reaches 118 to 120°C on a {{sugar-thermometer}}, the firm-ball stage. Remove from the heat. Stir in the {{vanilla-extract}}.

Beat the mixture vigorously with a wooden spoon until it begins to go from glossy to matte and thicken visibly, about 2 to 3 minutes. Unlike fudge, tablet sets quickly, so do not wait too long. As soon as it starts to hold its shape and look grainy around the edges, pour immediately into the prepared tray. Smooth the surface with the back of the spoon. Mark into squares with a knife before it fully sets; this takes about 5 minutes. Leave to set completely at room temperature for 2 hours, then break along the score lines.
